front: Work: the Roman patrician Marcus Vinicius falls in love with the Christian woman Lygia in this novel set in Nero's Rome
Work: this novel's title comes from a Latin phrase ('Where are you going?') allegedly said by Saint Peter to the risen Christ
Work: in this novel, Petronius, the 'Arbiter of Elegance,' helps his nephew Marcus Vinicius woo a barbarian hostage
Work: in this novel, the giant bodyguard Ursus breaks the neck of a bull/aurochs in the arena
Work: in this novel, the devious Chilo (Chilon Chilonides) helps the protagonist track down his love interest and later has his tongue torn out
Work: in this novel, Nero blames Christians for the Great Fire of Rome
Work: in this novel, Saint Peter leaves Rome, encounters the risen Christ, then returns to be martyred
Work: in this novel, the slave Eunice is in love with her master Petronius
Work: in this novel, Lygia is strapped nude to the back of a bull during arena games
Work: this trilogy of historical novels set in 17th-century Poland consists of With Fire and Sword, The Deluge, and Fire in the Steppe
Work: this novel fictionalizes the Khmelnytsky Uprising, a 17th-century Cossack revolt, and centers on the love between Jan Skrzetuski and Helena
Work: in this novel, Helena is rescued from the Tatars by Pan Longin
Work: in the second volume of The Trilogy, the character Kmita pines for Olenka and learns the Swedes were ordered by Radziwill to kill the Colonels
Work: in the final volume of The Trilogy, Michal Wolodyjowski dies protecting a gunpowder stash just before the Battle of Vienna
Work: in this novel, Danusia saves Zbyszko from execution after he attacks Kuno von Liechtenstein
back: The Teutonic Knights / Knights of the Cross (Henryk Sienkiewicz)

front: Author: this Polish author wrote a novel of manners in diary form about the decadent youth Leon, exemplifying positivism, whose protagonist commits suicide
Author: this Polish author wrote a children's novel featuring Nell and the Mahdi in Sudan
Author: this Polish author wrote a short story in which the title peasant child is whipped to death after sneaking into a noble manor to play a violin
Author: this Polish author wrote about an artist's travels in Paris with Pani Elzen and her sons Romulus and Remus
Author: this Polish Nobel laureate reportedly dandled Baron Wladyslaw Moes (inspiration for Mann's Tadzio) at a wedding when the boy was six, until the child urinated on his lap
